subject: The constraint underneath the presenting problem
body:
Most physician owners I work with can name their presenting problem — stalled growth, capacity ceiling, team dysfunction, revenue plateau.

But the constraint underneath that problem? That's harder to see.

An example:

A client came to me saying they needed better marketing. Revenue had plateaued. The schedule had gaps.

But when we mapped the constraint pattern, the issue wasn't demand generation. It was positioning.

They were competing on convenience and credentials. No unique point of view. No differentiated transformation. They sounded like everyone else in their market.

More ads would have brought more of the wrong patients. The constraint was upstream — who they were positioned to serve and what transformation they owned.

The diagnostic identifies what's constraining growth. The conversation identifies how to resolve it in your specific context.

— Dr. Negin Rajaipour, MD

subject: Why physician-owned practices fail to scale
body:
There's a predictable pattern in how physician-owned practices stall:

Phase 1: Founder dependency
The practice runs through you. Every decision. Every patient interaction. Every operational detail. Growth is limited by your capacity.

Phase 2: Team bottleneck
You hire help, but you're still the constraint. The team executes tasks but doesn't own outcomes. Delegation fails because systems don't exist.

Phase 3: Revenue ceiling
Volume increases but profitability doesn't. You're busier, not wealthier. The model doesn't scale because monetization is misaligned with leverage.

Most practices get stuck between Phase 1 and Phase 2. They hire before they build systems. They scale delivery before they fix positioning. They add capacity without redesigning the model.

The constraint shifts at each phase. What got you to $500K will not get you to $2M.

The question isn't whether to scale. The question is which constraint to address first.

— Dr. Negin Rajaipour, MD

subject: What separates this work from physician coaching
body:
I'm often asked how this advisory work differs from physician coaching or healthcare consulting firms.

Three distinctions:

1. Operator credibility
I'm not teaching from theory. I'm actively building a cash-pay telehealth practice. The strategies I recommend are the strategies I use. When I talk about AI-augmented delivery models or MSO structuring, it's from current operational experience — not case studies from five years ago.

2. Clinical judgment applied to business decisions
Physician training teaches pattern recognition, differential diagnosis, and decision-making under uncertainty. Those same faculties apply to practice strategy. I approach constraints the way I approach clinical cases — identify the underlying pathology, not just the presenting symptom.

3. Military operational discipline
Before medical school, I served in the U.S. Navy. That training shaped how I structure decisions, prioritize under pressure, and execute when the stakes are high. Engagements are direct, structured, and unsentimental.

The combination of these three lenses is rare. The work reflects all three.

— Dr. Negin Rajaipour, MD
Board-Certified Family Medicine · U.S. Navy Veteran

subject: The Resurrection Algorithm
body:
Earlier this year, I co-authored The Resurrection Algorithm™ with Brian Tracy and Medhat Zaki.

It became an Amazon #1 International Bestseller.

The book is about what happens after everything collapses — and how to rebuild from that edge with precision rather than hope.

It's part memoir, part framework. The clinical perspective meets the human cost of high-stakes decisions made under extreme pressure.

If you're navigating a transition that feels like a collapse — professional, structural, or personal — the book might resonate.

But more importantly: if you're at that edge right now, let's talk.

Reply to this email or submit a private inquiry.

— Dr. Negin Rajaipour, MD

subject: How we work together
body:
If you've been following these emails, you now understand how I approach physician advisory work:

Constraint-first. Identify the structural limit before designing the intervention.

Operator-informed. Strategic recommendations grounded in active practice-building experience.

Clinically precise. Pattern recognition applied to business decisions.

Engagements are structured around the specific decision or constraint you're navigating:

Private Strategic Sessions
60-minute or 90-minute focused sessions for single high-stakes decisions or transitions. Includes brief pre-call alignment.

Strategic Intensive (Half Day)
Four-hour private engagement for physician owners and healthcare executives navigating defined constraints. Includes pre-work diagnostic, working session, and written follow-up.

Ongoing Retainer Engagements
For healthcare practice development, AI integration strategy, MSO structuring, cash-pay transitions, and multi-phase strategic work.

I take on a small number of clients at a time. If you're ready to move forward, submit a private inquiry or reply to this email directly.

— Dr. Negin Rajaipour, MD
Board-Certified Family Medicine · U.S. Navy Veteran
Co-Author, The Resurrection Algorithm™
